coredata.dc:title: CuPc-Fe@BSA nanocomposite: Intracellular acid-sensitive aggregation for enhanced sonodynamic and chemo-therapy
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Bai, Qingchen
Wang, Miao
Wang, Kai
Liu, Jingwei
Qu, Fengyu
Lin, Huiming
coredata.dc:description: Due to their rigid π-conjugated macrocyclic structure, organic sonosensitizers face significant aggregation in physiological conditions, hindering the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS). An acid-sensitive nanoassembly was developed to address this issue and enhance sonodynamic therapy (SDT) and emission. Initially, copper phthalocyanine (CuPc) was activated using a H2SO4-assisted hydrothermal method to introduce multiple functional groups (–COOH, –OH, and −SO3H), disrupting strong π–π stacking and promoting ROS generation and emission. Subsequently, negatively charged CuPc-SO4 was incorporated into bovine serum albumin (BSA) to form CuPc-Fe@BSA nanoparticles (10 nm) with Fe3+ ions serving as linkers. In acidic conditions, protonation of CuPc-SO4 and BSA weakened the interactions, leading to Fe3+ release and nanostructure dissociation. Protonated CuPc-SO4 tended to self-aggregate into nanorods. This acidity-sensitive aggregation is vital for achieving specific accumulation within the tumor microenvironment (TME), thereby enhancing retention and SDT efficacy. Prior to this, the nanocomposites demonstrated cycling stability under neutral conditions. Additionally, the released Fe ions exhibited mimicry of glutathione peroxidase and peroxidase activity for chemotherapy (CDT). The synergistic effect of SDT and CDT increased intracellular oxidative stress, causing mitochondrial injury and ferroptosis. Furthermore, the combined therapy induced immunogenic cell death (ICD), effectively activating anticancer immune responses and suppressing metastasis and recurrence.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sonodynamic therapy
Acid-sensitive
Chemotherapy
Copper phthalocyanine
Immune activation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CuPc-Fe@BSA nanoparticles
In vitro therapy and ICD induction
Immune response and metastasis/ recurrence inhibition

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The FT-IR spectra of CuPc, CuPc-SO4 and CuPc-Fe@BSA. (B) Raman spectra of CuPc and CuPc-SO4. Fluorescence spectra of CuPc (C) and CuPc-SO4 (D) solution with different volume ratios of water/DMF (fw) (ex: 650 nm). ROS generation from CuPc (E) and CuPc-SO4 (F) solution with different fw using DCFH as indicator under US irradiation.

(A) The XRD patterns of CuPc, CuPc-SO4, and CuPc-Fe@BSA. (B) Zeta potential of CuPc, CuPc-SO4, CuPc-SO4@BSA, CuPc-Fe@BSA. (C) The high solution XPS spectrum of Fe in CuPc-Fe@BSA. (D) TEM images of CuPc, CuPc-SO4, an CuPc-SO4@BSA. (E) The hydrated ionic radius of CuPc-Fe@BSA in water. (F) The schematic diagram of assembly process for CuPc-Fe@BSA nanocomposite.

(A) 1O2 generation of CuPc-Fe@BSA with and without US irradiation using SOSG as dye. (Inset: ESR spectra of 1O2 radicals using TEMP as indicator). (B) 1O2 generation of CuPc-Fe@BSA under different pH conditions using SOSG as dye. (C) 1O2 generation (SOSG as dye) of CuPc-Fe@BSA in PBS 5.5 after soaking different periods. (D) TEM images and (E) XRD patterns of CuPc-Fe@BSA after soaking in different pH media and times. (F) 1O2 generation of CuPc-Fe@BSA before and after immersing in PBS 7.4 and 8 for 12 h using SOSG as dye. (G) Zeta potentials of CuPc@BSA, CuPc-SO4@BSA, and CuPc-Fe@BSA after immersing in pH 5.5, 6.8, and 7.4 for 12 h. (H)The release of Fe of CuPc-SO4@BSA under different pH media and times. (I) Transient sono-current responses of CuPc, CuPc-SO4, CuPc-Fe@BSA and CuPc-Fe@BSA after soaking in PBS 5.5 for 12 h. (J) The illustration of acid-responsive aggregation of CuPc-Fe@BSA.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of CuPc-Fe@BSA after soaking in different pH PBS for 12 h. (Inset: the corresponding photos). (B) Intracellular localization of CuPc-Fe@BSA after incubation for different times. (C) Absorption spectra of TMB after the treatment of CuPc-Fe@BSA and CuPc-SO4@BSA with/without H2O2. (D) ESR spectra of ·OH trapped by DMPO. (E) Absorption spectra of TMB after the treatment of CuPc-Fe@BSA + H2O2 under different pH. (F) GSH consumption under different concentrations of CuPc-Fe@BSA using DTNB as indicator. (G) The effect of GSH (different concentrations) on ·OH production using TMB as indicator. (H) Scheme of Fe3+/Cu2+-Fe2+/Cu+ cycle for GSH-ox and POD activity.

(A) Relative viabilities of L929, HeLa and 4 T1 cells after incubation with different concentrations of CuPc-Fe@BSA for 12 h. (B) Cell viabilities of 4 T1 cells treated with CuPc-Fe@BSA for 12, 24 and 48 h. (C) Viabilities of 4 T1 cells treated with CuPc-Fe@BSA (different concentrations) under US irradiation. (D) Viabilities of 4 T1 cells treated with different materials (20 μg/mL) under different pH conditions (control: normal DMEM). (E) Intracellular MDA content with treatment of CuPc-Fe@BSA under different conditions. Fluorescent images of (F) DCFH-DA, (G) Calcein AM, and (H) JC-1 staining 4 T1 cells for control, US, CuPc-Fe@BSA, and CuPc-Fe@BSA + US group. (I) Fluorescent images of intracellular HMGB1 distribution in 4 T1 cells under different treatments. (J) The flow cytometry analysis of CRT exposure in 4 T1 Cells after different treatments. Bio-transmission electron microscopy images of cells treated with CuPc-Fe@BSA under (K) normal cell medium and (L) acidic (pH 6.7) cell medium. (M) Bio-transmission electron microscopy images of 4 T1 cells from different treated groups. (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.01 and ***p < 0.001).

(A) Fluorescence images of mouse viscera after i.v. injection of CuPc-Fe@BSA for different times. (B) Treatment schedule for in vivo cancer therapy. (C) Relative tumor volumes and (D) bodyweights of tumor-bearing mice under different treatments. (E) Photographs of mice from different treatment groups and days. The corresponding (F) H&E and (G) TUNEL staining images of tumor. (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.01 and ***p < 0.001).

(A) The flow cytometry analysis of CD80 and CD86 expression in inguinal lymph nodes after different treatments. (i: Control, ii: US, iii: CuPc-Fe@BSA, and iv: CuPc-Fe@BSA + US) (B-D) Cytokines level of IFN-γ, TNF-α and IL-2 in plasma collected by using ELISA. (E) Pictures and (F) H&E staining images of lungs for each group. (G) Schematic diagram of recurrence simulation by 4 T1 cells injection. (H) Body weight of mice in normal and CuPc-Fe@BSA + US groups after 4 T1 cells injection. (I) Photographs and corresponding H&E staining of lung (a: control and b: CuPc-Fe@BSA + US).
Schematic diagram of acid-sensitive CuPc-Fe@BSA nanocomposite for synergetic SDT/CDT and anticancer immune activation.

head.ce:title: CuPc-Fe@BSA nanocomposite: Intracellular acid-sensitive aggregation for enhanced sonodynamic and chemo-therapy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Due to their rigid π-conjugated macrocyclic structure, organic sonosensitizers face significant aggregation in physiological conditions, hindering the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS). An acid-sensitive nanoassembly was developed to address this issue and enhance sonodynamic therapy (SDT) and emission. Initially, copper phthalocyanine (CuPc) was activated using a H2SO4-assisted hydrothermal method to introduce multiple functional groups (–COOH, –OH, and −SO3H), disrupting strong π–π stacking and promoting ROS generation and emission. Subsequently, negatively charged CuPc-SO4 was incorporated into bovine serum albumin (BSA) to form CuPc-Fe@BSA nanoparticles (10 nm) with Fe3+ ions serving as linkers. In acidic conditions, protonation of CuPc-SO4 and BSA weakened the interactions, leading to Fe3+ release and nanostructure dissociation. Protonated CuPc-SO4 tended to self-aggregate into nanorods. This acidity-sensitive aggregation is vital for achieving specific accumulation within the tumor microenvironment (TME), thereby enhancing retention and SDT efficacy. Prior to this, the nanocomposites demonstrated cycling stability under neutral conditions. Additionally, the released Fe ions exhibited mimicry of glutathione peroxidase and peroxidase activity for chemotherapy (CDT). The synergistic effect of SDT and CDT increased intracellular oxidative stress, causing mitochondrial injury and ferroptosis. Furthermore, the combined therapy induced immunogenic cell death (ICD), effectively activating anticancer immune responses and suppressing metastasis and recurrence.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Sonodynamic therapy (SDT), evolving from photodynamic therapy (PDT), has attracted significant interest in cancer treatment due to its non-invasive nature, deep tissue penetration (up to 10 cm), high efficacy, and precise control [1,2]. When subjected to ultrasound (US) stimulation, sonosensitizers generate reactive oxygen species (ROS), causing damage to cancer cells and tumor tissues [3,4]. Given the similarities between PDT and SDT, various photosensitizers have been employed as sonosensitizers, including porphyrins, xanthenes, phthalocyanines, TiO2, and ZnO [5–8]. Despite the benefits of organic sensitizers, such as their superior quantum yield, their tendency to undergo strong π–π stacking interactions leads to molecular aggregation in physiological conditions [9–12]. This aggregation results in the dissipation of excited energy through nonradiative processes, leading to decreased emission and ROS generation [13]. Termed the aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) effect, this phenomenon presents a significant obstacle for organic sonosensitizers [14,15].

In contrast to ACQ, aggregation-induced emission (AIE) molecules hardly emit in a monodispersed state, but they are highly emissive in an aggregated state. This unique property has diverse applications in bioimaging, theranostics, catalysis, and electrochemiluminescence (ECL) [16–18]. Recently, AIE PSs such as Ce6, hypericin, temoporfin, and TPA-Erdn have been employed, which significantly enhance photodynamic therapy (PDT) efficiency through their aggregation-induced effects [19–22]. Additionally, an AIE dye, 2-((5-(4-(diphenylamino)phenyl)thiophen-2-yl)methylene)malononitrile (TTMN), was incorporated into nanoparticles as a sonosensitizer for high-efficiency SDT [23]. Huang et al. developed an amphiphilic polymer (AIE-1) based on salicylaldazine, which was then assembled into sub20 nm micelles as sonosensitizers to effectively inhibit cancer growth and metastasis [24]. The aggregation arising from AIE agents holds promise for enhancing SDT efficiency even further. Furthermore, the size of sonosensitizer particles is crucial in SDT. Smaller nanoparticles typically show better diffusive mobility and tumor penetration, but their ability to remain in the tumor is limited. Therefore, the ideal sonosensitizers should maintain small particle sizes during circulation but transition into larger sizes after being taken up by cells. The normal physiological environment (pH 7.4) is more alkaline than the acidic tumor microenvironment (pH 6.5–6.8), with even greater acidity within cancer cells (pH 4.5–6.5) [25]. Given the in vivo pH variations, the acid-responsive aggregation of AIE-sensitizers has significant potential to promote specific SDT for cancer therapy. Despite this potential, research in this area remains limited.

Indeed, both ACQ and AIE mechanisms involve aggregation. AIE agents typically contain twisted groups whose rotation can disrupt the strong π–π stacking of planar conjugated structures [26–28].Essentially, the degree of close-packing in AIE is lower compared to ACQ. By conjugating with dendritic arms or bulky groups, specific functional ACQ molecules have been designed to interrupt aggregation; thus, transitioning to AIE behavior [29–32]. However, complicated organic reactions cannot be avoided. Encouraged by these previous reports, the ACQ photosensitizer copper phthalocyanine (CuPc) was activated by a simple H2SO4-assisted hydrothermal method to introduce many negative functional groups (CuPc-SO4). As expected, the activated treatment damaged the close π–π stacking to some degree to convert ACQ into AIE, favoring ROS (US irradiation) generation and emission. Subsequently, Fe3+ was utilized as a bridge to link the negatively charged CuPc-SO4 and BSA (pl = 4.7), resulting in the formation of CuPc-Fe@BSA nanoparticles (10 nm) with a small size [33]. In acidic environments, protonation of CuPc-SO4 weakens its interaction with Fe3+, causing the release of Fe3+ ions and consequent structural destabilization. Concurrently, CuPc-SO4 molecules tend to self-aggregate. This acidity-sensitive behavior enhances specific aggregation within cancer cells, promoting retention and enhancing SDT efficacy. In addition, the released Fe ions can serve as mimics of GSH oxidase (GSH-ox) and peroxidase (POD) to boost intracellular oxidative stress. This synergistic therapy not only has great anticancer effects but also stimulates an anticancer immune response to fight against metastasis and cancer recurrence (Scheme 1 ).

Unless specified otherwise, all of the chemicals were analytical grade and used without further purification. Calcein acetoxymethylester (calcein AM) was supplied by KeyGEN BioTCH. 3-(4,5-dimethyl-2-thiazolyl)-2,5-diphenyl-2-Htetrazolium bromide (MTT) was purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA), 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyroline-N-oxide (DMPO), 2,2,6,6-tetra-menthylpiperidine (TEMP), Copper (II) phthalocyanine (CuPc), iron nitrate nonahydrate, bovine serum albumin (BSA), 3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), ferrostatin, 5,5′-dithiobis(2-nitrobenzoic acid) (DTNB) and hydrogen dioxide (H2O2) were purchased from Aladdin Co. Ltd. SOSG-singlet oxygen sensor green reagent was purchased from Meilunbio. High mobility group box protein 1 (HMGB1), calreticulin (CRT) and JC-1 were purchased from Beyotime.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of samples were tested by Hitachi H-8100 at an accelerating voltage of 20 kV. Powder X-ray patterns (XRD) were tested on Rigaku Ultima IV diffractometer with Cu Ka radiation (40 kV, 20 mA). UV–Vis spectra were recorded on SHIMADZU UV2550 spectrophotometer. The fluorescence spectra were surveyed by HORIBA FL-3. The relative SDT experiments was carried out using LIPUSTIM Sonodynamic Therapy System (SDT-S019). The Flow cytometry assay data were obtained from MoFlo XDP. The Fluorescence imaging of viscera was obtained from IVIS Lumina. The hydrodynamic diameter and Zeta potential data was obtained from NanoBrook 90Plus PALS (5 repeats).

0.02 g of CuPc was added into 25 mL of DI water and 5 mL of concentrated sulfuric acid (18 mol/L). After ultrasound for 10 min, the solution was transferred into 50 mL of Teflon-lined autoclave and maintained at 200℃ for 24 h. The as-prepared sample was centrifuged (10000 r/min, 5 min), and washed with DI water for three times to remove sulfuric acid.

0.02 g of CuPc-SO4 and iron nitrate nonahydrate (0.007 g) was added in 10 mL of DI water (pH = 8) and ultrasound for 30 min. 0.1 g of BSA was dissolved in 10 mL of DI water, and then it was added into above CuPc-SO4-Fe solution and stirred for another 6 h. After that, the nanomaterial was centrifuged (10000 r/min, 5 min) and washed with water for three times to remove excessive iron ions.

SOSG was used as 1O2 probe. The mixture of CuPc-Fe@BSA (60 μg/mL, 980 μL) and SOSG solution (20 μL) was exposed to US irradiation (1 MHz, 50 % duty cycle, 1 W/cm2) for 5 min and the supernatant was collected by centrifugation at fixed intervals. The fluorescence intensity of the solution was detected by a fluorescence spectrophotometer.

DCFH was used to evaluate total ROS production. 1 mL of PBS (pH = 7.4) containing sample (60 μg/mL) and 100 μL of DCFH-DA solution was irradiated by US irradiation. Then, the supernatant was collected by centrifugation (10000 r/min, 5 min) and analyzed by fluorescence spectroscopy.

ROS production after soaking. Sample dispensing solution (60 μg/mL) was placed for different times, and then 1 mL of above solution was added in to 100 μL of DCFH-DA solution for US irradiation (5 min). Then, the supernatant was collected by centrifugation and analyzed by fluorescence spectroscopy.

Electron spin resonance (ESR) was used to confirm ∙OH and 1O2 by using 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyroline-N-oxide (DMPO) and 2,2,6,6-tetra-menthylpiperidine (TEMP) as spin trapping agent. Firstly, 35 μL of trapping agent was added to 1 mL of CuPc-Fe@BSA solution (100 μg/mL), and then it was transferred into ESR tube immediately after illuminated by US for 5 min.

The mixture of CuPc-Fe@BSA suspension and GSH (5 mM) was incubated at 37 ℃ for 10 min. At different intervals, 100 µL of supernatant was pipetted and diluted with 900 µL of PBS followed by adding 5,5′-dithiobis(2-nitrobenzoic acid) (DTNB, 10 µmol/mL, 100 µL) to detect GSH content using the absorbance at 412 nm.

CuPc-Fe@BSA (1 mg/mL), TMB (40 mM) and H2O2 (2.0 mM) were added to 1 mL of NaAC-HAC buffer solution (pH = 5.5) and incubated at 37 °C. The oxTMB formation was detected using VU-Vis absorption spectrum.

Electrochemical experiments were carried out using a three electrodes reaction cell on electrochemical workstation (CHI660D, Chenhua Instrument, Shanghai, China). A Pt wire was employed as counter electrode and Ag/AgCl was used as reference electrode. The preparation of working electrode for sonocurrent test was as follows: 2 mg of sample was dispersed in 1 mL of ethanol to obtain a suspension under ultrasound, and then it was smeared on a clean FTO glass surface and dried for 30 min in vacuum dryer. The coated area of FTO glass was 1.0 cm2. The sonocurrent test was carried out in 1.0 M Na2SO4 solution with a scan rate of 10 mV s−1.

L929 (fibroblast), 4 T1 (murine breast cancer), and HeLa (human cervical carcinoma) cells were cultured in DMEM (or RPMI 1640) including 10 % (v/v) fetal bovine serum (FBS) and penicillin/streptomycin (100 U/mL/100 mg/mL) in a humidified 5 % CO2 atmosphere at 37 °C.

For cytotoxicity test in vitro, cells (8 × 103 cells per well) were seeded into 96-well cell culture plates overnight to make sure attachment and then they were incubated with different concentrations of CuPc-Fe@BSA for different times. Afterwards, these media were removed and cells were incubated with fresh medium containing 0.5 mg/mL of MTT for another 4 h. The final medium was then replaced with 150 uL of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) per well and the absorbance (492 nm) was monitored using a microplate reader (WD-2102A). The cytotoxicity was expressed as the percentage of cell viability compared to untreated control cells.
The following fluorescence imaging was performed on Leica DFC450 C Microsystems.

For AM staining: 4 T1 cells were incubated with CuPc-Fe@BSA. After 12 h, cells were washed by PBS twice and fresh media was added. Then, these cells were irradiated by US (1 MHz, 50 % duty cycle, 1 W/cm2) for 5 min and stained with Calcein-AM for 30 min.

To evaluate intracellular acidic-enhanced emission, 4 T1 cells were incubated with CuPc-Fe@BSA (1 mL, 60 ug/mL) for 4 h to insure certain uptake. Then these cells were washed by PBS twice to remove non-uptake, and then fresh media (pH 6.7 or normal media) were added for another 2–6 h. DAPI was used to stain cell nucleus. After staining, all cells were washed by PBS for three times.

For intracellular ROS testing, DCFH-DA was used as indicator. Similarly, the cultured 4 T1 cells were treated with CuPc-Fe@BSA (60 μg/mL, 1 mL) for 12 h. After washing culture dish three times by using PBS, the cells were incubated with DCFH-DA (10 μmol/L) for 30 min and irradiated by US irradiation for 5 min.

The change of in mitochondrial membrane potential can indicate early apoptosis of cancer cells. 4 T1 cells were seeded on single orifice dishes at a density of 1.5 × 105 cells per well for 12 h. After incubating with CuPc-Fe@BSA (60 μg/mL, 1 mL) for another 12 h, they were irradiated by US for 5 min, and then cultured for another 2 h. The cells were stained with JC-1 (5 µg/mL) for 15 min, and then washed for three times with PBS.

All mice experiments were performed in compliance with the criteria of the National Regulation of China for Care and Use of Laboratory Animals. 4 T1 cells (10 6) were implanted under the left armpit of female Balb/c mice to establish tumor model. When tumor reached 50 mm3, the tumor-bearing mice were randomly divided into 6 groups: (1) Saline (control), (2) US, (3) CuPc-Fe@BSA, and (4) CuPc-Fe@BSA + US, 5) CuPc-SO4 + US, and 6) CuPc-SO4@BSA + US group. After tail intravenous injection, (2) and (4–6) groups were irradiated with US irradiation (1 MHz, 1 W/cm2, 50 % duty for 3 min). After different treatments, the tumor volume and body weight of each group was measured every two days.

After 14 d of treatment, heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney, and tumor tissue in control and CuPc-Fe@BSA + US groups were collected for histological analysis. After a series of treatments, the encased tissues were sliced and stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) for analysis using optical microscope.

To study the maturation of dendritic cells, inguinal lymph nodes were harvested from different treatment groups. Cell suspensions were prepared, washed with PBS (pH 7.4), and filtered through a 200-mesh cell sieve. Cells were then stained with anti-mouse CD80FITC and anti-mouse CD86 PE for flow cytometer (FC) analysis. To detect the immunomodulatory cytokines, blood was collected and centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 10 min to obtain serum. The contents of TNF-α, IFN-γ, and IL-2 in serum were measured using ELISA kit (Dakewe Biotech Co., Ltd.).

Quantitative data are expressed as mean ± s.d. Statistical comparisons were conducted by using Student’s two-sided t-test as *P < 0.05 (significant), **P < 0.01 (moderately significant) and ***P < 0.001 (highly significant).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CuPc-Fe@BSA nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: Initially, treating CuPc in an H2SO4 solution introduces various functional groups, including C-OH (1056 cm−1), −SO3 (1211 cm−1), –COO (1536 cm−1), and NO (1560 cm−1) (Fig. 1 A). Furthermore, the metal–ligand (Cu–N) vibration band at 873 cm−1 [34] notably diminishes in CuPc-SO4, indicating disruption of the Cu–N bond due to the H2SO4 treatment. However, CuPc-SO4 still maintains its characteristic phthalocyanine (Pc) skeleton (700–1600 cm−1) overall [35]. XPS spectra also reveal the emergence of CS (288.8 eV) [36], C-SOx (169.2 eV) [37] and CO bonds (403.9 eV) [38] as shown in Figure S1. The increase in O content from 9.82 % to 18.53 % further confirms the oxidation of CuPc (Table S1). Elemental mapping images of CuPc-SO4 demonstrate a uniform distribution of C, N, O, S, and Cu elements (Figure S2). Despite this, CuPc-SO4 still exhibits the characteristic Raman signals of CuPc, as depicted in Fig. 1B. [39,40]. Nonetheless, the weakening of macrocyclic vibration (at 598 and 683 cm−1), pyrrole stretching (at 1336 cm−1), and isoindole ring stretching (at 1449 cm−1) peaks, coupled with the decrease of N content from 16.21 % to 7.91 % (Table S1), suggests that the activation treatment may damage the Pc framework. Additionally, dimethylformamide (DMF) and water serve as good and poor solvents, respectively, regulating the aggregation of the samples. As illustrated in Figure S3, both CuPc-SO4 and CuPc display broad and low Q-bands in water, indicating aggregation [41]. From Fig. 1C-F, with increasing water content (fw), CuPc exhibited ACQ behavior, while CuPc-SO4 exhibited great ROS generation and emission. Jeon et al. synthesized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) of different sizes and reported that GQDs with smaller particle sizes exhibited greater emission in the aggregated state owing to their lower π-conjugation [42]. Artesunate was attached to ZnPc to create conjugates aimed at enhancing sonodynamic activity (AESA) through aggregation [43]. Sun et al. developed a covalent organic framework (COF-919) using planar and twisted AIEgen-based motifs to reduce layer-layer interactions, resulting in enhanced emission and ROS generation [44,45]. X-ray diffraction was used to further determine the changes in the accumulation structure. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the diffraction signals of CuPc-SO4 change remarkably. The main diffraction peak of CuPc-SO4 at 23.5° indicates a larger interplanar space (0.76 nm) in comparison with that of CuPc (29.3°, 0.61 nm). According to the aforementioned investigations, a simple activation strategy is to change the aggregation structure and dominate the transformation from ACQ to AIE.

After the combination of Fe3+ and BSA, the emerging amide I (1651 cm−1), amide II (1537 cm−1), and C-Hx(2874, 2925, and 2967 cm−1) bands can be attributed to BSA (Fig. 1A). In addition, the Fe-O (542 cm−1) and Fe-N bonds (1000 cm−1) further demonstrated the successful combination of Fe ions [46,47]. To understand the assembly process, the zeta potentials of the samples were investigated. As depicted in Fig. 2B, CuPc-SO4 exhibits a lower potential (−18.76 ± 2.66 mV) than CuPc (−8.80 ± 1.66 mV), which is attributed to the presence of negatively charged active groups (–OH, –COO-, −SO3 -). After modification with BSA, the surface potential decreased to −18.92 ± 0.63 mV due to the lower isoelectric point of BSA (pI = 4.7). The co-assembly of Fe3+ introduces a positive charge of 11.03 ± 1.19 mV. Analysis using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) confirms the presence of Fe (Figure S4 and S5). The Fe 2p spectrum reveals peaks for Fe2+ (708.5 and 721.5 eV) and Fe3+ (714.5 and 728.5 eV) with a Fe2+/Fe3+ ratio of 2.4:1 (Fig. 2C). From Fig. 2D, both CuPc and CuPc-SO4 exhibit the characteristic rod morphology of Pc crystals, several micrometers in length. However, CuPc-Fe@BSA appears to be a small nanoparticle (10 nm), and an analysis of the hydrated ionic radius confirms its even size distribution (Fig. 2E). The presence of XRD peaks in CuPc and CuPc-SO4, attributed to the π-π stacking of Pc rings, is absent in CuPc-Fe@BSA (Fig. 2A). From the investigation, the negative charge of CuPc-SO4 provides numerous active sites for Fe3+ binding, facilitating their assembly into BSA (negative charge) and disrupting π-π aggregation. As illustrated in Fig. 2F, acting as charge-linkers, Fe3+ ions connect negative BSA and CuPc-SO4 to form the CuPc-Fe@BSA nanocomposite. Without Fe3+, CuPc-SO4@BSA also exhibits some nanorods and similar XRD peaks to CuPc-SO4 (Figure S6). Hence, both Fe3+ and BSA are crucial for achieving the final small particle size and excellent dispersibility (Figure S7).

CuPc-Fe@BSA was utilized as a sonosensitizer, and its effectiveness was assessed using singlet oxygen sensor green (SOSG) reagent as a dye. As depicted in Fig. 3 A, clear fluorescent emission at 530 nm indicates ROS generation when CuPc-Fe@BSA is exposed to US irradiation. Another ROS indicator, 2,7-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA), was also used to confirm US-induced ROS (Figure S8). Electron spin resonance (ESR) analysis was conducted to identify the active ROS species, showing the typical signal of singlet oxygen (1O2) using 2,2,6,6-tetra-menthylpiperidine (TEMP) as an indicator (inset of Fig. 3A). As presented in Fig. 3B, ROS production under acidic conditions exceeds that under basic conditions. Moreover, ROS generation increases with prolonged immersion times (PBS 5.5) (Fig. 3C). To understand the performance, the morphology and structure of CuPc-Fe@BSA during the process were examined. After soaking in PBS 5.5 for 6 h, the nanoparticles aggregate (Fig. 3D). With further extension to 12 h, some nanorods appear, and XRD peaks also emerge (Fig. 3E), indicating the re-aggregation of Pc molecules. Under weak basic conditions (PBS 7.4), the sample maintains small nanoparticles with minimal diffraction peaks after 24 h (Fig. 3D-E). Consequently, there is no significant increase in ROS generation after storage in PBS 7.4 and 8.0 for 12 h (Fig. 3F).

To further elucidate the acid-sensitive mechanism, the change in zeta potential was recorded. As revealed in Fig. 3G, when CuPc-Fe@BSA was immersed in neutral or weakly basic media for 12 h, the surface potential was still positive (11.03 ± 1.19 or 9.87 ± 1.46 mV), and there was no significant change compared with that of the primary sample (11.03 ± 1.19 mV) to illustrate the stability under nonacidic conditions. However, the potential was −13.21 ± 1.26 mV at pH 5.5, reflecting the change in the nanocomposite. With an abundance of hydroxyl and acid radicals, the protonation of CuPc-SO4 weakens its electrostatic interaction with Fe ions, leading to their release. This acid-triggered Fe ion release is further confirmed by ICP analysis, as illustrated in Fig. 3H. Specifically, the total Fe content of CuPc-Fe@BSA, analyzed via treatment with aqua regia for 24 h, is 58.97 ± 3.83 µg (ICP analysis). This suggests that the Fe release ratio reaches 87.01 % (51.31 ± 3.45 μg) at pH 5.5 after 24 h, while only 2.07 % (1.22 ± 0.33 μg) Fe release is observed at pH 7.4. Furthermore, the Fe release results in the negative charge of CuPc-SO4 and BSA, facilitating their separation due to electrostatic repulsion. Driven by π-π interactions and decreased solubility (from protonation), CuPc-SO4 molecules tend to accumulate under acidic conditions (Fig. 3J). After immersion of CuPc-Fe@BSA in pH 5.5 for 12 h, only three XRD peaks emerge, indicating preferred orientation and stratiform Pc molecules (Fig. 3E). The equal intervals of these peaks further support this observation. It is noteworthy that the diffraction pattern also differs from that of CuPc-SO4, likely due to the pH value and the presence of Fe ions and BSA. This new structure enhances transient ultrasonic current and increases US-generated charges, leading to greater ROS generation (Fig. 3I and B-C). Based on the aforementioned investigation, this active strategy not only disrupts π-π stacking to convert from ACQ to AIE but also introduces abundant active groups (hydroxyl and acid radicals) to achieve acid-sensitive behavior. The weak acidic condition in TEM promotes specific aggregation in cancer cells, thereby enhancing intracellular retention and ROS production.

In view of the acid-induced accumulation, the emission behavior of CuPc-Fe@BSA was studied after 12 h in media with different pH values. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the strongest fluorescence was observed in the presence of PBS 5.5, and the acidity-triggered AIE was further visualized by corresponding photos (Fig. 4A inset). In addition, the intracellular AIE was also investigated. First, 4 T1 cells were incubated with CuPc-Fe@BSA for 4 h to ensure uptake. Then, these cells were washed 3 times to remove extracellular samples, and new media (pH 6.7) was added for further incubation. Fig. 4B reveals the obvious increase in intracellular red fluorescence with incubation time, which is attributed to acid-promoted aggregation.

In addition, the Fenton catalytic activity of CuPc-Fe@BSA was investigated using 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) as an indicator. From Fig. 4C, the absorption peak at 652 nm (ox-TMB) verifies ·OH generation from Fenton catalysis, and this behavior can also be attributed to peroxidase (POD) activity. Some ox-TMB absorption of the CuPc-SO4@BSA group is ascribed to residual Cu ions. After the co-assembly of Fe ions, more ·OH is produced. Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra further verified the presence of the active species of ·OH using 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline n-oxide (DMPO) as a captor, which can react with ·OH to form DMPO-OH, revealing four characteristic peaks with an amplitude of 1:2:2:1 (Fig. 4D). As shown in Fig. 4E, CuPc-SO4@BSA exhibited acid-enhanced catalytic activation, which was attributed to acid-induced Fe release. In addition, the consumption of glutathione (GSH) was also surveyed via the 5,5′-dithiobis-2- nitrobenzoic (DTNB) assay because GSH can break the SS bond in DTNB to form 2-nitro-5-mercaptobenzoic acid (TNB), which is yellow in color. Typically, from Fig. 4F, the absorption of TNB (412 nm) decreases gradually with increasing CuPc-Fe@BSA concentration, resulting in GSH depletion. The performance can also be described as that of GSH oxidase (GSH-ox) because Fe3+/Cu2+ can oxidize GSH to Fe2+/Cu+, which can further contribute to Fenton catalysis. Therefore, a certain amount of GSH is added to increase ·OH generation (Fig. 4G). GSH-ox activity can not only deplete GSH to inhibit reductive stress but also improve lower valence ion (Fe2+/Cu+) conversion to enhance Fenton catalysis. The redox cycle of Cu/Fe ions determines dual-enzyme activity (POD and GSH-ox) to further boost ROS levels (Fig. 4H).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro therapy and ICD induction
ce:section.ce:para: The in vitro anticancer effect was assessed via the MTT assay. Initially, cancer (4 T1 and HeLa) and normal (L929) cells were co-incubated with different concentrations of CuPc-Fe@BSA for 12 h. From Fig. 5 A, when the sample concentration increased to 60 µg/mL, the viability of the 4 T1 and HeLa cells decreased to 60.81 ± 2.03 % and 62.36 ± 3.63 %, respectively. As anticipated, the viabilities of 4 T1 cells treated with CuPc@BSA and CuPc-SO4@BSA were greater than those of cells treated with CuPc-Fe@BSA due to their larger sizes and lower POD activities (Figure S9-10). However, L929 cells maintained 88.51 ± 1.86 % viability under the same conditions, indicating substantial biocompatibility with normal cells. The selective cytotoxicity toward cancer cells can be attributed to the weak acidity and high expression of H2O2 in the TME. Following a 48-hour incubation period, the viability of 4 T1 cells decreased to 44.59 ± 8.26 % (60 μg/mL, Fig. 5B). Upon US irradiation, dose-dependent cytotoxicity was evident, with cell viability further dropping to 6.40 ± 1.30 % (60 μg/mL) due to the combination of CDT and SDT (Fig. 5C). To assess the impact of aggregation on viability, CuPc-Fe@BSA, CuPc-SO4, and CuPc-SO4@BSA (20 μg/mL) were co-incubated with 4 T1 cells for 10 h to ensure uptake, followed by a medium change to fresh pH 6.7 or normal (pH 7.6) DMEM medium for an additional 8 h. As illustrated in Fig. 5D, cell viability was 62.05 ± 1.27 % at pH 6.7 due to acidity-enhanced CDT. Viability further declined to 5.60 ± 1.77 % with US irradiation, lower than that in normal medium (37.05 ± 1.27 %). The significant SDT performance under acidic conditions is ascribed to acidity-triggered aggregation. Furthermore, bio-TEM images revealed numerous nanoparticles in cells cultured in normal DMEM (weak alkalinity). However, some nanorods were observed under weakly acidic culture conditions, confirming their intracellular aggregation (Fig. 5K-M).

To explore the therapeutic mechanism of CuPc-Fe@BSA, intracellular ROS production was surveyed by using DCFH as an indicator. As shown in Fig. 5F, there was no obvious fluorescence in the US and control groups. Some green fluorescence in the CuPc-Fe@BSA group is due to CDT derived from POD activity. As expected, the CuPc-Fe@BSA + US group exhibited remarkable green fluorescence, indicating increased ROS generation from SDT. Due to interference from the red fluorescence of the sample, cell viability was visualized by calcein-AM single-staining only. Almost all cells in the control and US groups exhibited green calcein fluorescence. After the addition of CuPc-Fe@BSA, green fluorescence was barely visible in the CuPc-Fe@BSA + US group, indicating mass mortality due to the combination of CDT and SDT (Fig. 5G). Lipid peroxidation (LPO) is considered a significant indicator of ferroptosis [48,49]. Here, intracellular malondialdehyde (MDA), a typical end product of LPO, was analyzed. From Fig. 5E, compared with those in the control and US groups, the MDA content in the CuPc-Fe@BSA group was greater because of the CDT effect. After US was introduced, the increase in the MDA content was attributed to additional ROS generation from SDT. When a ferroptosis inhibitor (Ferrostatin, Fer-1) was added, the MDA concentration decreased markedly, further demonstrating ferroptosis. In addition, the mitochondrial membrane potential (MMP) was also analyzed by the JC-1 assay. JC monomer (green) fluorescence can be found in the CuPc-Fe@BSA group, suggesting a decreased MMP (Fig. 5H). The CuPc-Fe@BSA + US group displayed bright green fluorescence, indicating severe mitochondrial dysfunction with the lowest MMP. Moreover, mitochondrial damage can also release mitochondrial reactive oxygen species and further activate the ferroptosis pathway to exert anticancer effects [50,51].

Immunogenic cell death (ICD) can stimulate immune defense, which is vital for the inhibition of recurrence and metastasis [52,53].To evaluate the ICD level in vitro, the translocation of high mobility group box protein 1 (HMGB1) was surveyed first (Fig. 5I). According to the immunofluorescence images, HMGB1, which was originally located within the nucleus region, was noticeably released after CuPc-Fe@BSA + US treatment. The calreticulin (CRT, a chaperone protein in endoplasmic reticulum) exposure level also increased from 0.54 % (control) and 1.49 % (US) to 24.3 % (CuPc-Fe@BSA) and 34.0 % (CuPc-Fe@BSA + US) as shown in Fig. 5J, respectively. HMGB1 release can signify dendritic cell activation and proinflammatory cytokine secretion. CRT exposure, a well-known “eat-me” signal, is considered an ICD indicator [54,55]. The high ICD of the CuPc-Fe@BSA + US group was attributed to synergetic CDT + SDT, which boosted the subsequent immune response.

In vivo fluorescence imaging was conducted to assess the distribution of the CuPc-Fe@BSA nanocomposite. Fig. 6 A shows that the fluorescence signals were predominantly concentrated in the tumor and lungs. Within 12 h, the fluorescent signal reached a maximum in the tumor, indicating excellent accumulation, likely due to in situ aggregation within tumor cells. Given the specific cytotoxicity of CuPc-Fe@BSA + US, we investigated its in vivo antineoplastic efficacy. The 4 T1 bearing female BALB/c mice were divided randomly into six groups: 1) Control, 2) US (1 MHz, 1 W/cm2, 50 % duty, 3 min), 3) CuPc-Fe@BSA, 4) CuPc-Fe@BSA + US, 5) CuPc-SO4 + US, and 6) CuPc-SO4@BSA + US. After the injection (i.v.) of sample solution for 12, 24, and 48 h, US treatment was carried out (Fig. 6B). The weights of the mice and the volumes of the tumors were recorded every 2 days. As exhibited in Fig. 6C and Figure S11, the body weight in each group gradually increased during treatment, indicating the negligible side effects of the samples and US. The tumors in the CuPc-Fe@BSA + US group almost disappeared owing to the acid-boosting effect of CDT and SDT (Fig. 6D). However, those in the control and US groups increased to 6-fold compared with those in the control group. The CuPc-Fe@BSA group exhibited a certain inhibitory ability, indicating that a single CDT was not enough to inhibit tumors. In addition, the CuPc-SO4 + US and CuPc-SO4@BSA + US groups showed negligible inhibition ability because of their large sizes and low CDT/SDT activities. The corresponding treatment efficiency is visualized from photos as exhibited in Fig. 6E. Furthermore, H&E- and TUNEL-stained tumor images also revealed significant tumor damage and apoptosis in the CuPc-Fe@BSA + US group (Fig. 6F-G). In addition, H&E staining of the heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney was carried out to detect significant differences between the treatment and control groups (Figure S12). As shown in Figure S13, all indicators in routine blood analysis were within normal ranges. The above investigation revealed that CuPc-Fe@BSA + US has excellent anticancer performance and biocompatibility and has great potential as an anticancer agent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Immune response and metastasis/ recurrence inhibition
ce:section.ce:para: The ICD always releases specific damage-associated molecules that are subsequently recognized by antigen-presenting cells, triggering an immune response [56].Dendritic cells (DCs), which are typical antigen-presenting cells, play a vital role in stimulating innate and adaptive immunity [57,58]. Upon exposure to antigens, immature DCs undergo maturation and disperse throughout the body, with notable accumulation in the lymph nodes and spleen. After 3 days of treatment, the inguinal lymph of the mouse was extracted to analyze CD80 and CD86 (DCs maturation indicator) levels using flow cytometry. The highest DC maturity in the CuPc-Fe@BSA + US group (20.10 %) was attributed to the greatest ICD (Fig. 7 A), which is consistent with the in vitro dataas exhibited in Fig. 4. Mature DCs further present certain major histocompatibility complexes (MHC) to active T cells, which secrete abundant cytokines for immune regulation [59]. Here, mouse serum was also collected, and the levels of related immunomodulatory cytokines, including tumor necrosis factor (TNF-α, cellular immunity mediator), interferon γ (IFN-γ, important immune responses/immunosurveillance cytokine), and interleukin-2 (IL-2, immune regulatory lymphokine), were analyzed by using an enzyme-linked immune sorbent assay (ELISA). From Fig. 7B-D, compared with those of the control group, the CuPc-Fe@BSA + US group exhibited increases of 4 (IFN-γ), 2 (IL-2), and 6 (TNF-α) times, confirming that synergistic therapy can efficiently stimulate immune activation.

After treatment for 14 days, these mice were fed for another 30 days. No recurrence or lung metastasis was found in the CuPc-Fe@BSA + US group (Fig. 7E-F). In addition, new normal (without tumor) and treated mice (CuPc-Fe@BSA + US group after treatment for 44 days) were i.v. injected with a 4 T1 cell suspension (10 5) to simulate tumor recurrence, as shown in Fig. 7G. After 14 days, the new control group exhibited many metastatic nodules on the lung, while these nodules were not detected in the CuPc-Fe@BSA + US group (Fig. 7I). In addition, the body weights of the treated group increased slightly, but those of the control group clearly decreased after 6 days, indicating a poor physiological state (Fig. 7H). On account of the above findings, CuPc-Fe@BSA + US not only exhibited strong antitumor effects on CDT and SDT but also efficiently promoted anticancer immunity for metastasis and recurrence inhibition.

Past research has predominantly focused on addressing CuPc's poor solubility by incorporating hydrophilic segments or loading it onto nanocarriers for cancer treatment [60–62]. In this study, CuPc-Fe@BSA nanoparticles were engineered to demonstrate acid-sensitive aggregation, enhancing SDT/CDT and imaging. The effective strategy begins with CuPc activation using H2SO4, a straightforward method disrupting its π-conjugated structure and π–π stacking, thereby transitioning from ACQ to AIE for improved aggregation-induced emission and ROS generation [63]. Additionally, Fe3+ ions acted as linkers connecting negatively charged CuPc-SO4 and BSA to form CuPc-Fe@BSA nanoparticles (10 nm), ensuring notable dispersion stability in neutral environments. Under acidic conditions, protonation of CuPc-SO4 and BSA weakened their interaction with Fe3+, leading to Fe ion release and nanostructure disintegration. Subsequently, protonated CuPc-SO4 molecules tended to reassemble into nanorods (2 μm). This pH-sensitive aggregation behavior, stemming from AIE, proved vital for specific retention and cytotoxicity in cancer therapy [27]. Furthermore, released Fe ions exhibited POD and GSH-ox activity for CDT. The combined SDT/CDT approach displayed significant anticancer efficacy, stimulating immune responses against metastasis and recurrence.

Qingchen Bai: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Miao Wang: Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Kai Wang: Methodology, Investigation. Jingwei Liu: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Fengyu Qu: Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Huiming Lin: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial supports for this study were provided by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21571045), Natural Science Foundation of Heilongjiang Province of China (TD2020B001), and Excellent Scientific Research Team Project of Harbin Normal University (XKYT202001).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2024.05.113.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
